caveats: a: No exact NAICS 111336 occupation or task distribution was found, and the provided labor-share input is missing.
a: Mechanization and AI are related but not identical; equipment productivity is relevant only where it substitutes current work or avoids hiring.
a: Task exposure varies sharply by fruit softness, tree architecture, terrain, fresh-versus-processing destination, and existing mechanization.
rho: USDA technology evidence spans research projects, demonstrations, and broad precision tools with different commercial readiness.
rho: Farm internet access does not establish usable field connectivity, integrated data, or implementation capability.
rho: Weather and crop variability can make results from one orchard system fail to transfer to another.
e: The code is defined by a moving crop-revenue mix, so a farm can change classification after price or yield shifts without changing its underlying assets.
e: The provided lower-middle-market firm count is missing and must not be inferred from broad farm counts.
e: Family farms may separate land, equipment, labor, packing, and crop sales across related legal entities.
s5: The source covers specialized fruit, tree-nut, and berry farms, not the exact combination code or EBITDA band.
s5: Producer counts include up to four people per farm, so producer age is not a farm-level transfer probability.
s5: A land sale, lease, or family succession may not transfer a standalone operating company with customers and workforce.
q: No public evidence directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled farm labor savings.
q: Commercial structure differs by crop, market window, processing destination, cooperative membership, certification, and storage life.
q: Yield gains can reduce market prices and should not be confused with retention of a fixed gross benefit.
d5: The acreage and sales evidence covers all fruit, tree-nut, and berry production, not combination farms.
d5: Sales growth is nominal and mixes price, yield, acreage, quality, and commodity composition.
d5: Permanent crops face large geographic and crop-specific risks that an aggregate range can conceal.
o: The operator can change while the orchard and quantity persist, especially through leases or management contracts.
o: Import substitution reduces domestic operator-required quantity and is also reflected in demand durability.
o: Robotics may centralize management across farms but does not remove accountable land and crop operations.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue comes from repeated seasonal crop sales through spot markets, cooperatives, marketing contracts, processors, packing houses, wholesale channels, retailers, foodservice buyers, and direct-to-consumer outlets. Price depends on crop, grade, timing, yield, contract terms, storage, certification, and market conditions; diversified farms can sell several crops across different harvest windows.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive internal units, non-control financing vehicles, passive farmland lessors, hobby or subsistence farms, custom farm-labor and harvesting contractors, packing or processing businesses without qualifying crop production, farms outside the screened EBITDA band, and operations whose production concentration places them in another fruit or tree-nut NAICS industry are excluded.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market farm operators that grow a combination of fruits and tree nuts, with no one fruit family or tree-nut family accounting for half of agricultural production value, and repeatedly supply external packers, processors, cooperatives, wholesalers, retailers, foodservice buyers, or consumers while retaining accountable responsibility for land, crops, labor, compliance, and delivery.

a.bridge: USDA measures labor dependence, mechanization constraints, and specific automation capabilities across broader specialty-crop, fruit, tree-nut, orchard, and vineyard populations rather than the wage-weighted share of current unautomated tasks in NAICS 111336. I translate evidence on crop scouting, intelligent spraying, yield estimation, harvest robotics, and farm administration into task exposure while discounting physical pruning, picking, irrigation repair, equipment work, and quality-sensitive handling. Research demonstrations and larger-farm technologies likely overstate exposure for smaller diversified operations, while the absence of exact combination-farm occupation data may understate digital planning work created by multiple crops.
caveats: No exact NAICS 111336 occupation or task distribution was found, and the provided labor-share input is missing.
Mechanization and AI are related but not identical; equipment productivity is relevant only where it substitutes current work or avoids hiring.
Task exposure varies sharply by fruit softness, tree architecture, terrain, fresh-versus-processing destination, and existing mechanization.
a.change_evidence: Conduct wage-weighted time-and-task studies at at least twenty qualifying mixed fruit-and-nut farms, recording crop mix, current automation, seasonal worker hours, scouting, spraying, harvest, packing, office work, and vacancies avoided; obtain commercial deployment and utilization data from orchard-technology vendors.
a.rationale: AI can substitute or avoid hiring in crop and pest scouting, image-based yield estimates, labor and harvest planning, irrigation and input recommendations, spray targeting, recordkeeping, compliance drafts, purchasing, marketing analysis, and customer communication. Soft-fruit handling, pruning, training, selective harvest, field repair, food-safety execution, and many terrain-sensitive tasks remain physical, although robotics and intelligent equipment can expose part of that work.

d5.bridge: USDA reports broader fruit, tree-nut, and berry acreage and nominal sales changes between agricultural censuses, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket of NAICS 111336 farms. I use acreage growth and the sector's large continuing receipts as quantity-direction evidence, then discount nominal sales growth and widen for crop mix, bearing lags, yields, imports, weather, disease, and the classification threshold. Acreage can overstate output when yields fall and understate it when mature orchards or improved varieties raise yields.
caveats: The acreage and sales evidence covers all fruit, tree-nut, and berry production, not combination farms.
Sales growth is nominal and mixes price, yield, acreage, quality, and commodity composition.
Permanent crops face large geographic and crop-specific risks that an aggregate range can conceal.
d5.change_evidence: Build a weighted crop basket for verified combination farms and track constant-quality farmgate volumes, bearing acreage, yields, removals and plantings, contracted orders, inventories, imports, exports, and per-capita availability using USDA Quick Stats and ERS yearbook tables.
d5.rationale: Fruit and nuts serve durable fresh, processed, snack, beverage, ingredient, and export demand, and broader producing acreage increased between the last two censuses. Diversification can soften a single-crop shock. Domestic quantity faces import competition, water constraints, disease, extreme weather, maturation delays, changing diets, and crop-specific oversupply; combination farms can also leave this NAICS code when one crop becomes dominant.

caveats: The code is defined by a moving crop-revenue mix, so a farm can change classification after price or yield shifts without changing its underlying assets.
The provided lower-middle-market firm count is missing and must not be inferred from broad farm counts.
Family farms may separate land, equipment, labor, packing, and crop sales across related legal entities.
e.change_evidence: Construct an exact NAICS 111336 farm universe from Census of Agriculture microdata or USDA lists, then verify crop-revenue shares, external buyer history, normalized EBITDA, land and operating-company separation, active status, and control rights through tax records, contracts, and operator interviews.
e.rationale: A farm already in the lower-middle-market band will usually qualify as a recurring external supplier because commercial fruit and nut production is repeatedly sold to nonfarm buyers. Exclusions remain for passive farmland structures, captive growing units, operations without transferable accountable production, businesses whose crop concentration moves them to another NAICS code, and farms whose apparent scale does not produce normalized EBITDA in the band.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The operator can change while the orchard and quantity persist, especially through leases or management contracts.
Import substitution reduces domestic operator-required quantity and is also reflected in demand durability.
Robotics may centralize management across farms but does not remove accountable land and crop operations.
o.change_evidence: Track acres shifted to contract farming, buyer-owned production, passive leases, custom-management arrangements, imports, autonomous equipment, farm closures, and NAICS reclassification in the verified combination-farm universe.
o.rationale: Almost all surviving domestic fruit and nut quantity still requires an accountable farm operator to control land access, crop plans, irrigation, labor, pest and food-safety practices, harvest, and buyer delivery. Software, robotics, and custom contractors can change staffing without eliminating farm accountability. Quantity can leave the lens through imports, buyer vertical integration, passive leasing to another operator, or conversion to a single dominant crop classified elsewhere.

q.bridge: USDA market-news and price-spread sources document transparent spot, shipping-point, wholesale, retail, contract, volume, quality, and import conditions across specialty crops, not the discounted share of an implemented AI benefit retained by combination farms. I infer limited retention where buyers can benchmark prices and imports expand, with greater retention for differentiated quality, organic or local channels, storage timing, direct sales, scarce varieties, and fixed-price contracts. Broad fresh-produce evidence may understate retention for storable tree nuts and overstate it for perishable fruit under buyer concentration.
caveats: No public evidence directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled farm labor savings.
Commercial structure differs by crop, market window, processing destination, cooperative membership, certification, and storage life.
Yield gains can reduce market prices and should not be confused with retention of a fixed gross benefit.
q.change_evidence: Review buyer and cooperative contracts, settlement sheets, grade deductions, packing and marketing charges, price formulas, renewal outcomes, direct-sales mix, storage decisions, crop-specific import exposure, and realized margins before and after measurable productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Many growers are price takers in transparent, seasonal markets, and productivity can encourage additional supply that lowers price. Packers, processors, cooperatives, and retailers can capture gains through contract renewal, grades, marketing charges, and negotiated prices, while imports cap domestic pricing. Diversification, differentiated crops, direct channels, and contracts allow some benefit to remain with the operator.

rho.bridge: USDA reports precision-agriculture adoption by broad crop-farm size and documents specialty-crop automation research and commercial intelligent spraying, not five-year implementation of exposed AI tasks at combination farms. I translate demonstrated commercial tools and high internet availability into implementation potential, then discount for small-market equipment economics, seasonal utilization, heterogeneous canopies, fragmented records, connectivity, capital constraints, food-quality risk, and older operators. Broad large-farm adoption data likely overstate implementation for smaller family farms, while lower-middle-market farms may be larger and more adoptive than the overall specialty-crop population.
caveats: USDA technology evidence spans research projects, demonstrations, and broad precision tools with different commercial readiness.
Farm internet access does not establish usable field connectivity, integrated data, or implementation capability.
Weather and crop variability can make results from one orchard system fail to transfer to another.
rho.change_evidence: Survey verified qualifying farms annually on AI and automation deployment by task, acreage covered, crop, capital cost, seasonal utilization, error and damage rates, worker acceptance, and realized hours; obtain installed-base and renewal data from precision-spray, vision, robotics, and farm-management vendors.
rho.rationale: Planning, recordkeeping, market analysis, scouting, and advisory workflows can adopt quickly through software, and commercial precision spraying shows that sensor-guided systems can work in orchards. Full harvest automation is slower because crops and field conditions vary and damage can destroy value. Integration across several crops adds both useful data and complexity, so implementation depends on farm scale, orchard design, connectivity, and management discipline.

s5.bridge: The 2022 Census of Agriculture measures producer age and family-farm status for specialized fruit, tree-nut, and berry farms, not control transfers of eligible NAICS 111336 lower-middle-market operators. I translate the high older-producer share into five-year succession pressure, then discount for multiple producers per farm, intra-family succession, land sales without a transferable operating company, partial interests, leases, and closures. The broader specialty population includes many very small farms, likely overstating age pressure relative to qualifying firms, while perennial-crop asset intensity can make succession especially consequential.
caveats: The source covers specialized fruit, tree-nut, and berry farms, not the exact combination code or EBITDA band.
Producer counts include up to four people per farm, so producer age is not a farm-level transfer probability.
A land sale, lease, or family succession may not transfer a standalone operating company with customers and workforce.
s5.change_evidence: Match the eligible universe to producer age, ownership shares, successor participation, land tenure, entity structure, estate planning, orchard age, and prior sales; obtain farm and permanent-crop transaction records from brokers, lenders, county deeds, and agricultural appraisal firms.
s5.rationale: Forty-two percent of producers in the broader specialty group were at least 65 and the average age was 59.6, creating meaningful succession pressure. Yet 93 percent of farms were family farms, so transfer may occur within the family, through land or orchard parcels, or by lease rather than a qualifying control sale of the operating company.

principal_driver: High specialty-crop labor dependence and multi-crop planning complexity create credible opportunities in vision, forecasting, targeted inputs, scheduling, and administration.
principal_weakness: Farm availability is unmeasured, physical work is difficult to automate across heterogeneous crops, and much of a transition's value may reside in land rather than a transferable operating company.

run_meta.title: Fruit and Tree Nut Combination Farming

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments growing a combination of fruits and tree nuts with no fruit family or tree-nut family accounting for half of agricultural production value, and identifies concentration-based cross-references.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 111336 Fruit and Tree Nut Combination Farming

sources.supports: Reports broader sector farms, acreage and sales changes, producer age, family-farm share, internet access, hired labor, economic classes, expenses, income, and geographic concentration.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture Highlights: Fruit, Tree Nut, and Berry Production

sources.supports: Reports that wages, salaries, and contract labor represented 40 percent of fruit and tree-nut production expenses in the 2022 Census of Agriculture and describes hired farm occupations and labor-force constraints.
sources.title: Farm Labor

sources.supports: Documents high specialty-crop labor dependence and USDA programs supporting automation and mechanization in production, harvesting, and processing.
sources.title: Developing Automation and Mechanization for Specialty Crops

sources.supports: Reports commercial orchard testing and deployment of intelligent spraying, including reductions in drift, ground loss, pesticide use, and chemical cost, demonstrating a practical sensor-guided workflow.
sources.title: Reducing Labor and Advancing Precision Agriculture through Automation

sources.supports: Shows that precision-agriculture adoption rises sharply with farm size, reports high use of selected technologies at larger crop farms, and identifies labor saving among adoption motives.
sources.title: Precision Agriculture Use Increases With Farm Size and Varies Widely by Technology

sources.supports: Documents labor-intensive orchard tasks, mechanization constraints, rising labor costs, import competition, changes in domestic production, and crop-specific differences in harvest mechanization.
sources.title: U.S. Fruit and Vegetable Industries Try To Cope With Rising Labor Costs

sources.supports: Describes frequent public reporting of specialty-crop prices, volumes, quality, condition, shipping-point, wholesale, retail, movement, and import-market information used by buyers and sellers.
sources.title: Specialty Crops Market News

sources.supports: Reports the broader sector's continuing farm cash receipts and identifies USDA time series for acreage, production, prices, trade, stocks, and per-capita use.
sources.title: Fruit and Tree Nuts

title: Fruit and Tree Nut Combination Farming
